metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fifth test to AC6 that patches `applied_ids` (or the underlying file read) to count invocations, calls `maybe_emit_pending_hint` N>1 times on the same tracker, and asserts the read is performed exactly once regardless of N.
findings.finding: AC1 claims '.ensure-applied is read AT MOST ONCE per store per process' and 'a converged store caches the empty set and every later write is O(1), zero further reads'. None of the four tests in AC6 explicitly verifies the read-count invariant — the tests cover warning emission and suppression but not whether `applied_ids` (or its underlying file read) is called more than once across multiple invocations.
findings.suggested_fix: AC6(e): 'After the first call to `maybe_emit_pending_hint(tracker)`, subsequent calls (with the same tracker, regardless of pending/converged state) invoke `applied_ids` zero additional times — verified by asserting the call count of a `unittest.mock.patch` on `applied_ids` equals 1 after 5 invocations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit fallback rule for `.ensure-hinted` analogous to the `.ensure-applied` degradation spec: e.g. 'if absent or unparseable, treat as epoch 0 (i.e., the hint is past-due — fire it)'.
findings.finding: The fallback decision rule for `.ensure-hinted` when the file is absent (first invocation) or contains unparseable garbage is not specified — the plan defines a detailed degradation protocol for `.ensure-applied` (absent/garbage/non-list → empty set) but provides no analogous rule for `.ensure-hinted`, leaving the 'first-ever invocation' behavior and the garbage-parse path unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Emission + rate-limit' paragraph; contrast with 'Pending computation' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Emission + rate-limit' paragraph: 'If `.ensure-hinted` is absent or its content cannot be parsed as a float, treat the last-hinted time as 0 (epoch), so the hint fires on the first invocation against any pending store.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `.ensure-hinted` absence or unparseable content degrades to 'no prior hint' (treat timestamp as epoch/0), consistent with the `.ensure-applied` degradation rule, so a corrupt file triggers an immediate re-emit rather than silencing hints.
findings.finding: The plan specifies explicit garbage/absence degradation for `.ensure-applied` (absent/garbage/non-list → empty set), but does NOT specify how the `.ensure-hinted` timestamp reader should behave when the file is absent or contains unparseable content. Under the fail-silent wrapper, a parse failure at the timestamp-read step silently suppresses all subsequent hints for that store (for the entire process lifetime), because the pending-set IS cached (non-empty) but every attempt to check the rate-limit throws and is swallowed — permanently silencing the nudge rather than treating absence as 'no prior hint'.
findings.location: 'Emission + rate-limit' section; AC line 3 (`ensure.hint_interval_secs`); compare `.ensure-applied` degradation spec in 'Pending computation' section
findings.suggested_fix: In `maybe_emit_pending_hint`, wrap the `.ensure-hinted` timestamp read in a try/except that catches all parse/IO errors and falls back to `last_hinted = 0.0` (epoch). This mirrors the `.ensure-applied` degradation pattern and ensures the function either emits a hint or updates `.ensure-hinted`, rather than silently skipping. Document this degradation rule in the AC (parallel to the `.ensure-applied` spec in AC1).
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact on-disk format of `.ensure-hinted` (e.g., 'a single UTC Unix epoch as a decimal float string, no trailing newline, written with `fsutil.atomic_write(…, encoding="utf-8")`') so the write, parse, and comparison steps are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-hinted` file format (the on-disk representation of 'a single timestamp') is never defined — the rate-limit decision rule 'last-hinted timestamp is older than hint_interval_secs' requires parsing this value, but the plan specifies neither the format (Unix epoch float, integer seconds, ISO-8601 string, etc.) nor the encoding, leaving the implementer to invent an arbitrary format and the comparison protocol underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Emission + rate-limit' paragraph: '`.ensure-hinted` contains a single line: the Unix epoch timestamp as a decimal float string (e.g. `1720000000.123`), written as `str(time.time())` and parsed with `float(content.strip())` for the rate-limit comparison.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact `.ensure-hinted` file format in the plan (e.g. 'a single Unix epoch float as a UTF-8 decimal string, e.g. `1718000000.0\n`') so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-hinted` timestamp format is never specified (ISO 8601 string, Unix epoch float, integer seconds, etc.). An executing agent must invent a format, which risks interoperability with any future reader/writer of this file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: '`.ensure-hinted` contains a single line: the POSIX timestamp as a `float` rendered by `str(time.time())`, e.g. `"1718000000.123456"`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or extend test (a)) that exercises the WARNING emission path through a real `write_and_push` call on a pending store — i.e., an integration-level check that the hook wiring actually fires, not just that `maybe_emit_pending_hint` works in isolation.
findings.finding: No test verifies that the WARNING is actually emitted when a REAL `write_and_push` completes successfully on a pending store — tests (a)–(c) appear to call `maybe_emit_pending_hint` directly, so the hook wiring in `write_and_push` could be silently absent and the suite would still pass green.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6(a), change the description to: '(a) … triggered via a real `write_and_push` call on a minimal pending store, emits exactly one WARNING naming pending units + `rebar fsck --repair`, and re-emits only after the interval.' This makes the wiring itself part of the acceptance gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or explicitly gate) that WS1 has landed `rebar._store.ensures` with the exact exports WS2 depends on (`REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids()`, `APPLIED_MARKER`) before WS2 work begins; the module does not currently exist in `src/rebar/_store/`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'WS1 landed the registry as `rebar._store.ensures` and exports, on that module: `REGISTRY_IDS` (the frozen id tuple), `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]` … and `APPLIED_MARKER`' — treating these as existing codebase symbols that WS2 will import. A directory listing of `src/rebar/_store` and exhaustive grep searches for `ensures.py`, `REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `APPLIED_MARKER`, and `ensure-applied` all return zero hits in source. The module `rebar._store.ensures` does not exist. WS2's entire implementation (the `maybe_emit_pending_hint` compute, the `pending = registry_ids() - applied_ids(tracker)` formula, the module-level cache, the `APPLIED_MARKER` constant, and the no-import-cycle AC) depend on these WS1 symbols as external imports.
findings.location: What section ('WS1 landed the registry as `rebar._store.ensures`…') and AC1, AC5, Scope (In: `maybe_emit_pending_hint` in `rebar._store.ensures`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite block to the plan: 'Requires WS1 merged (confirm `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` present with `REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `APPLIED_MARKER` exports before starting WS2).' Alternatively, if this plan IS WS1+WS2 combined, move the ensures-module creation into WS2's Scope/In list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 'Prerequisite: WS1 must be merged and `rebar._store.ensures` available with exports `REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids()`, `APPLIED_MARKER`' statement to the Scope section, and verify the WS1 symbol names/signatures before finalising the WS2 plan.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` and all its named exports (`REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids()`, `APPLIED_MARKER`) do not exist in the current codebase (`src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py`). The plan frames WS1 as already 'landed', but WS2 has a hard unacknowledged blocker: it cannot be implemented until WS1 ships `ensures.py`. The Scope section should explicitly state this prerequisite, and the plan should not describe WS1 symbols as if they are currently present.
findings.location: What section ('WS1 landed the registry as `rebar._store.ensures`'); Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Prerequisite (hard blocker): `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` (WS1) must be merged and export `REGISTRY_IDS: tuple[str, ...]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`, and `APPLIED_MARKER: str` before any WS2 implementation begins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/config.py` to the Scope's In-set as an explicit edit target (one line: `from rebar._config_schema import EnsureConfig as EnsureConfig`), consistent with the re-export pattern for all other `*Config` classes.
findings.finding: `config.py` is an unenumerated edit target. The plan specifies 'a re-export from config.py' for `EnsureConfig`, but `config.py` is not listed in the Scope section's In-set. Every existing `*Config` class has an explicit `from rebar._config_schema import FooConfig as FooConfig` re-export line in `config.py` (lines 31–40); `EnsureConfig` requires the same treatment or `from rebar.config import EnsureConfig` will fail for any existing consumer.
findings.location: What section ('Config' subsection: 'a re-export from config.py'); Scope section (In: list omits `config.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope 'In:' list, append: '`config.py` (add `from rebar._config_schema import EnsureConfig as EnsureConfig` re-export, matching the pattern at lines 31–40)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or handle) the accepted in-process cache staleness after `fsck --repair`: either note it as an explicit accepted limitation in the What/Scope section, or add a `clear_pending_cache(tracker)` hook that WS3's repair path can call to invalidate the module-level dict entry for the repaired tracker.
findings.finding: The module-level pending-set cache is per-process and never invalidated. After WS3's `fsck --repair` updates `.ensure-applied` on disk within the SAME process (e.g., a long-running MCP server or batch agent that calls both a repair and subsequent writes), the in-process cache still holds the pre-repair pending set — so the hint continues to fire (rate-limited) for the remainder of that process session even though the store is now converged. The plan is silent on this post-repair in-session staleness.
findings.location: What section — 'Pending computation (compute-once, per process)' + 'a converged store caches the empty set'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line `clear_pending_cache(tracker: str) -> None` function to `rebar._store.ensures` that does `_pending_cache.pop(canonical_tracker(tracker), None)`. Document in the Scope that WS3's `fsck --repair` must call this after writing `.ensure-applied`. This keeps the compute-once guarantee intact for the common path while ensuring the hint silences promptly after an in-session repair.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) inline the WS1 interface contract for `rebar._store.ensures` (the four exported symbols with their signatures and exception semantics) into WS2's 'What' section so an executor has a self-contained spec, or (b) add an explicit 'Depends on WS1 landing ensures.py with the following interface: …' block.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` is referenced throughout WS2 as an existing module exporting `REGISTRY_IDS`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids()`, and `APPLIED_MARKER`, but the module does not exist in the codebase — it is WS1's deliverable. WS2's plan provides no specification of the interface contract it depends on, so an executor implementing WS2 in isolation (or if WS1 is incomplete) has no defined protocol to code against.
findings.location: What section — 'WS1 landed the registry as `rebar._store.ensures`'; AC1; AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'canonical tracker path' precisely in the plan (e.g. '`os.path.realpath(tracker)`') so the cache-key normalization is unambiguous.
findings.finding: 'Canonical tracker path' (the module-level cache key) is not defined — the plan does not specify whether it means `os.path.abspath`, `os.path.realpath`, or some other normalization. If two call sites pass the same logical path with different representations (relative vs absolute, symlink vs target) the cache misses and `.ensure-applied` is read more than once.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Cache key = `os.path.realpath(tracker)` — this is computed once inside `maybe_emit_pending_hint` before the cache lookup.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification command for AC4, e.g. `grep -E 'hint_interval_secs|hint_enabled|REBAR_ENSURE_HINT' docs/config.md` confirming the section and both env-var names appear.
findings.finding: AC4 requires the `[ensure]` section and its two env vars to be documented in `docs/config.md`, but the plan provides no verification step or proving command to confirm that documentation was written and is correct — no grep, no doc-test, no CI check is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Verified by: `grep -cE "hint_interval_secs|hint_enabled" docs/config.md` returns ≥ 2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the accepted-gap rationale (inline committers excluded from `write_and_push` hook by design, because the nudge is not a correctness mechanism) in a persistent location — a `# NOTE:` block in `event_append.py`, a CLAUDE.md entry, or a named sub-section of `docs/config.md` — so future contributors do not treat the exclusion as a bug.
findings.finding: The accepted-gap architectural decision (inline committers — claim/transition/compact/delete — are intentionally excluded from the hint hook) is described in the plan body but is not called out for capture in any persistent design document (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or a dedicated section of docs/config.md). Future maintainers reading the code may perceive the exclusion as a bug rather than a deliberate trade-off.
findings.location: § Where it hooks — accepted gap paragraph; Scope §; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Design notes` sub-section to the `[ensure]` entry in `docs/config.md` (or a block comment near the hook site in `event_append.py`) that names the accepted-gap mutators and states the rationale: 'The nudge is advisory; correctness does not depend on it firing on every write.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `docs/config.md` `[ensure]` section, document `.ensure-hinted`: its single-timestamp format, that deleting it resets the rate-limit (forcing the next covered write to re-evaluate), and that it is git-ignored and safe to remove.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-hinted` file — its single-timestamp format, how to reset it manually, and what deleting it implies — is not called out as needing documentation. The plan specifies that `docs/config.md` will cover 'the [ensure] section + its two env vars' but does not mention documenting the file artifact itself, leaving operators who encounter this file in their worktree without a reference.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'State files' note under `[ensure]` in `docs/config.md`: '`.rebar/.ensure-hinted` — written by rebar after emitting a pending-hint; contains a single ISO-8601 timestamp. Delete this file to force an immediate re-evaluation on the next write. It is git-ignored and never committed.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
